ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers spring rolls generically; no specific Vietnamese fried spring roll sub-entry exists. Closest parent used.

preparation_method: wrapped in lace rice paper (bánh tráng rế)
filled with minced pork, shrimp, glass noodles, and vegetables

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Cha Gio Re is 4 pieces, weighing approximately 200 g, as typically served at Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Spring Roll, Fried (Chun Juan) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table; adapted for Vietnamese cha gio re profile
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (FoodData Central) and FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Vietnamese Cha Gio Re (lace rice paper variant) exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entry for fried spring rolls (NDB 35185 / similar composite entries) and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA data for Southeast Asian fried spring rolls. The lace rice paper (bánh tráng rế) wrapper is thinner than standard wheat-based wrappers, resulting in slightly lower carbohydrate and slightly higher fat per 100 g due to deep-frying absorption. Values represent a best-estimate composite for a pork-and-shrimp filled, deep-fried rice-paper spring roll.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Rice-Based and Mixed Fried Snacks — analogous estimate based on fried rice paper rolls and mixed-filling spring rolls
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese Cha Gio Re in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 52 is estimated from the closest analogous entries: fried spring rolls with mixed fillings and rice-based wrappers, which cluster in the GI range 48–58 in the Sydney database. The lace rice paper wrapper (bánh tráng rế) is made from rice flour and has a lower starch density than wheat wrappers, supporting a GI estimate at the lower end of this range. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 47.6) / 100 ≈ 24.7, where carbohydrate per 200 g serving = 23.8 × 2 = 47.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~840 mg per 4-piece serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of Cha Gio Re due to its high sodium content from fish sauce and seasoning; a 4-piece serving provides approximately 840 mg sodium, representing 37% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (GL ~24.7 per serving) and refined carbohydrate from rice paper wrapper
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Cha Gio Re in moderation; the estimated GI of 52 (low category) is partially offset by a high glycemic load per serving (~24.7) due to the large portion size, and the deep-frying process adds significant fat that may slow gastric emptying but also contributes excess calories.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (13.5 g/100 g), saturated fat (3.2 g/100 g), cholesterol (~52 mg/100 g), and sodium (~420 mg/100 g) from deep-frying and seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Cha Gio Re intake; deep-frying significantly increases total and saturated fat content, and the high sodium load from fish sauce and seasoning compounds cardiovascular risk, making this an occasional-only food within a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cha Gio Re (Chả Giò Rế) is a Vietnamese deep-fried spring roll distinguished by its unique lace-patterned rice paper wrapper (bánh tráng rế), which creates an exceptionally crispy, delicate, and visually distinctive golden-brown exterior when deep-fried. The filling typically consists of minced pork, shrimp, glass noodles (mung bean vermicelli), wood ear mushroom, carrot, and taro, seasoned with fish sauce, garlic, and pepper. Unlike standard Vietnamese cha gio (which uses smooth rice paper), the rế variant uses a woven or lace-style rice paper that produces a lighter, crunchier shell with a more intricate texture. In Singapore, this dish is served at Vietnamese restaurants as a starter or snack, often accompanied by nuoc cham dipping sauce (a sweet-sour-spicy fish sauce condiment) and fresh lettuce and herbs for wrapping. Nutritionally, each 4-piece serving (~200 g) provides approximately 496 kcal, 18.4 g protein, 27 g fat, and 47.6 g carbohydrate. The dish is calorie-dense due to deep-frying, with moderate protein from pork and shrimp. Sodium content is high (~840 mg per serving) from fish sauce and seasoning, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The estimated glycemic index is 52 (low category), but the glycemic load per serving is moderate-to-high (~24.7), requiring portion control for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with crustacean or fish allergies.

What is the difference between cha gio and cha gio re?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Vietnamese Cha Gio Re exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central fried spring roll composite data and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA regional data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using closest fried rice-paper spring roll entries. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central fried spring roll entries and FAO/INFOODS SEA data; no exact Cha Gio Re entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. 2. The lace rice paper (bánh tráng rế) wrapper is thinner and more delicate than standard rice paper, which may result in slightly different fat absorption during deep-frying compared to standard spring rolls; values reflect a best-estimate composite. 3. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Cha Gio Re was found. 4. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data for this food category. 5. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data for this food category. 6. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source data for this food category. 7. Halal status is marked false as the dish contains pork; it may be prepared with halal-certified pork substitutes at some establishments, but the default formulation is not halal. 8. The serving size of 200 g (4 pieces) is based on the GrabFood listing from Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant, Circular Road, Singapore. 9. GL per serving = (52 × 47.6) / 100 ≈ 24.7, where carbohydrate per 200 g serving = 23.8 × 2 = 47.6 g.
